Gent lemen :
The City of Lancaster and the Lancaster Redevelopment
Agency are currently undertaking the implementation of
redevelopment plans for six separate redevelopment projects
which will require negotiating, drafting and approving
agreements with developers anil property owners for the
development of property within the redevelopment project areas,
the acquisition of real property through negotiation or eminent
clomain proceedings and the issuance of bonds, notes or other
forms of indebtedness to provide the necessary funds for such
redevelopment activities. 
In addition the City and Agency have
initiatecl proceedings for the adoption of a redevelopment plan
for a seventh redevelopment project area. The City and the
Agency have entered into contracts for Iegal services with the
Iaw firm of Stradling, Yocca, Carlson & Rauth (the "Firm").
while the legaI services are provided by a number of attorneys
employed by the Firm, the Firm and the Agency,/City have agreecl
that David McEwen, a shareholder in the Firm, wilI be primarily
responsible for providing such services and wilI act as City
Attorney and Agency Counsel. The attorneys provi.ding Iegal
services hereunder are collectively referred to as "CounseI".
AlI fees are paid directly to the Firm.
On behalf of the City of Lancaster, the Lancaster
Realevelopment Agency and ourselves, we are requesting a formal
opinion on the following questions:
ATTORNEYS AT LAW




Fair PoIitical Practices commission
October 7, 1992
Page Two
1. 
May Counsel participate in a governmental decision by
provicling advice to the City./Agency or by otherwise providing
Iegal services pursuant to the existing professional services
contracts between the City,/Agency anal the Firm in connection
with the drafting anil negotiating of agreements relating to the
implementation of the redevelopment projects, if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the Firm wi]1 receive a fee for
such services?
2. 
May Counsel participate in a governmental ilecision by
provicling advice to the Ci.ty,/Agency or by otherwise providing
legal services pursuant to the existing professional services
contracts in connection with the proceedings for the acloption
of a proposed redevelopment project area, if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the Firm will be asked to provide aclclitional
legal services in connection with the implementation of the
redevelopment plan?
3. 
May Counsel participate in a governmental clecision by
proviCling advice to the CiLy,/Agency or by otherwise provicling
legaI advice pursuant to the existing professional services
contracts concerning threatened or pencling litigation, 
if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the Firm will receive a fee or be
asketl to provide additional legal services in connection with
such l it igation?
4. 
May Counsel participate in a governmental clecision by
providing advice to the City,/Agency or by otherwise provialing
legal services pursuant to the existing professional services
contract concerning bonds, notes or other debt obligations to
be issuecl by the City,/Agency, if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the Firm will receive a fee for such services?
5. 
May Counsel negotiate in their private capacity with
the City,zAgency concerning a modification or amendment to the
existing professional services contracts between the
City,/Agenc, and the Fi rm.
B ackg rou nd
Professional Services Aq reement s .
In 1979 the Agency entered into a professional services
contract (the "Agency Agreement" ) with the Firm. The Agency
Agreement provides for the Firm to provide lega1 services


Fair Political Practices Cornrnission
October 7, L992
Page Three
through Counsel for the Agency in connection with all aspects
of the Agency's activities. 
These activities include, among
others, the rendering of advice anil 1ega1 opinions in
connection with the adoption of redevelopment plans for
realevelopment project areas, the negotiation, drafting and
rendering lega1 advice concerning disposition and development
agreements ("DDA's"), owner participation agreements ("OPA's")
and other similar agreements which are necessary for the
implementation of the redevelopment pIans, the handting of
Litigation on behalf of the Agency and the rendering of legal
services in connection with debt obligations incurred by the
Agency. The Iega1 services provided in connection with debt
obligations of the Agency are generally referred to as "bond
counsel services. " Bond counsel services include providing
advice on the applicable laws affecting the issuance of debt
obligations, draftinq the necessary resolutions anal lega1
documents and rendering an opinion to the City or Agency, upon
which the purchasers of the debt obligations may rely,
concerning, among other thinqs, the legality of the
proceedings, the enforceability of the obli.gation and whether
or not interest on such debt obliqations is excludable from
gross income for federal tax purposes.
The Agency Agreement was subsequently amended on June 2,
1980, August 2, 1982, and JuIy 3, 1989 to reflect changes in
feesl however, the substance of the Agency Agreement remains
unchanged. The Agency Agreement, including the amendments
thereto, is attached hereto as Exhibi.t A.
ln 1987, pursuant to the City's request for proposals, the
City entered into a professional services contract (the ,,City
Agreement") with the Firm pursuant to which the Firm would
provide legal services to the City as City Attorney. The City
Agreement incorporated by reference the existing Agency
Agreement. The City Agreement was amended on January 19, 19gg
and July 3, 1989 to modify the fee arrangement. The City
Agreement including the amendments thereto, is attacheal hereto
as Exhibit B.
Both the Agency Agreement and the Ci.ty Agreement designate
David McEwen ("City Attorney/Agency Counse1,,) as the attorney
primarily responsible for providing legal services to the
Agency and the City, but contemplate that such services will be
provided by many different attorneys emptoyed by the Firm baseal


Fair PoliticaI Practices Commission
October 7, ),992
Page Four
on their areas of expertise and availability. 
Mr. McEwen is
responsible for coordinating these legal services on behalf of
the Fi rm.
Pursuant to the Agency Agreement and the City Agreement,
the fees to be paid are determined on the basis of the type of
service being provided. Under the City Agreement, the Firm
receives a monthly retainer which covers all scheduled meetings
and routine lega1 services. Services provided in connection
with litigation, 
administrative proceedings and special
projects are based on an hourly rate. 
Under the Agency
Agreement, with the exception of services rendered in
connection with bond issues which are bi1led on the basis of
schedules set forth in the Agreement, all services are billed
on an hourly basis. The fee schedules for various types of
bond issues are typical of the fees charged by other firms
providing such services and reflect the amount of work required
to provide such service and the risks associated with rendering
opinions in connection with issuance of tax exempt securities
upon which third parties will re1y. In the event the boncls are
not issued, the lega1 services are to be compensated for on an
hourly basis.
AII of the fees paid under the City Agreement and Agency
Agreement are paid to the Firm and nothing is paid directly to
Counsel. The City Attorney/Agency Counsel and the other
attorneys providing services receive a salary from the Firm.
Those that are shareholders also receive a distribution of the
Firm's profits derived from all of its clients but are not
entitleal to any pro rata share of the fees paid by the Agency
or City. 
A11 of the shareholders of the Firm, which inctudes
the City At to rney,/Agency Counsel, own less than 10% of the
shares of the Fi rm.
The Redevelopment process.
. Redevelopment is a process requiring both public and
private participation governed by the piovisioi-rs of the
Community Redevelopment Law (HeaIth and Safety Code Section
33000 e! seq.), The redevetopment process tegins wfren tfre City
Council determines to-activate the Agency witiin tne-Ciiy. -
While the City Council may appoint a-sepirate body to act as
the agency board, frequently Lrre city c6uncil upp-oints-iiselr
(Health ancr safetv code seclion 33204) to act ai-ttre ase;;y-


Fair Po1iticaI Practices Commission
October 7, L992
Page Five
board. The Agency is a separate legal entity, but is
authorizecl to contract with the City to have City employees
provide the necessary staff services for the Agency. (Health
anal Safety Code Section 33126).
The redevelopment process can be divided into two distinct
phases: the plan adoption phase and the implementation phase.
The plan adoption process for each redevelopment plan is a
lengthy, complicated process which involves actions by the City
Council, the Planning Commission and the Agency. It includes a
fiscal review process, which requires the Agency to meet with
all of the affected taxing entities to address the fiscal
impacts of the proposed redevelopment plan on such entities.
It is frequently the case that if the Agency cannot come to an
agreement with each of the affected taxing entities, 
such
entities will challenge the adoption of the redevelopment plan
by fiting litigation.
Once the adoption of the redevelopment plan is completed
and all legal challenges disposed of through further
negotiation or court action, the Agency then moves into the
implementation phase, This process can take 20 to 30 years anal
in some cases even longer to complete. This phase requires the
active participation of the Agency and private enterprise.
while this cooperative effort can take a variety of courses,
generally the Agency's involvement will require either Iand
acquisition, the construction of public improvements or both.
Private enterprise constructs the residential, commercial or
industrial buildings depending upon the type of redevelopment
being undertaken. Typically, the transaction will 
be
negotiateal between the Agency and the landowner and/or
developer anal the agreement will be embodied in either a DDA or
an OPA. After approval of the DDA or OpA, the Agency will be
required to fu1fi11 its obJ"igations uncler the agreement, This
may require further negotiations for land acquisition or, in
some cases, the initiation 
of eminent domain proceedings. This
process is repeated many times throughout the life of [ne
redevelopment project.
In order to provide revenues for the Agency to fulfill 
its
redevelopment obrigations, california constitulion artlcie xvr,
section 16 and Health and safetv code sectio" asiio pio"iJe"
that tax revenues generated ry trre i-ncreases in asseised vi:.ue
of property in the project area over the assessed value of such


Fair Political Practices Comrnission
October 7, L992
Page Six
property prior to the adoption of the redevelopment plan are to
be allocated to the Agency for the purpose of repaying debt.
Therefore, to be entitled to the tax increment revenues, the
Agency must incur debt. TypicalIy, agencies incur debt through
the issuance of bonds or notes as provided in Hea]th and Safety
Code Section 33540 et seq. In order to market such securities,
the Agency must provide an opinion upon which the thiril party
purchasers of the securities may rely that the transaction is
valid and, in most cases, that the interest on the securities
is excluded from gross income for purposes of federal income
tax purposes. Such an opinion must be rendereil by an attorney
or firm of attorneys with expertise in the area of tax exempt
financing and whose opinion is recognizeal anal acceptable to the
purchasers of the securities. 
The Firm has such expertise and
is a nationally recognized bond counsel.
It should also be noted that many of these issues were
raised in a validation proceeding brought by the Agency in
connection with a proposed bond issue. The Court in this
contested action concluded that no conflict of interest existed
in connection with the Firms participation in the approval of
the bond issue. A copy of the Judgment is attached hereto as
Exhibit D.
Ana lvs i s
Ouestions Relatinq to Participatj.on In Governmental
Deci sions .
Questions I through 4 deal with specific fact situations,
but can be reduced to a single, general question:
May an attorney participate in a local government
clecision by providing legaI services pursuant to an
existing professional services contract between the locat
governmental entity and the f i.rm of which the attorney is a
partner, shareholder or employee, if as a resutt of such
participation, the firm will receive a fee for such
services or it is reasonably foreseeable that
implementation of the decision wiII require additional
legal services for which the firm will receive a fee?
We believe the answer to this question is that the attorney
may properly provide the services which the firm has contracted
to provide and may provide such services on a continuing basis


Fair PoIitical Practices Commission
October 7, L992
Page Seven
without being disqualified merely as a result of the fees being
paid to the firm. This conclusion applies equally to the
specific questions I through 4 presented above and is baseal on
three separate modes of analysis. First, the decisions which
Counsel is required to participate in to fulfill 
the Firm's
contractual obligations merely impLement a prior ilecision of
the Agency and the City to undertake the redevelopment of
portions of the City and to contract with the Firm for legal
services in connection with such redevelopment program.
Second, Government Code Section 82030(b)(2) excluiles "salary"
received from a government agency. As the FPPC has previously
determined, the term "salary" as used in this section is to be
broadly defined to include legal fees for services provided to
a governmental agency. (See Ritchie Letter, a copy of which is
attacheal as Exhibit C). Since the fees received by the Firm
from the Agency are "salary" and therefore are not inclutletl in
income for purposes of the Act, such fees should not be
consiclered when determining whether or not a decision of the
Agency wiIl have a material financial effect on the Firm.
Finally, if the attorney was required to disqualify himself or
herself from participating in each decision for which the Firm
would receive a fee, the Agency would be unable to obtain legaI
services on a contractual basis from any outside, private
attorney. Therefore, even if there were a technical violation
of the Act, the rule of necessity, as codified in Government
Code Section 87I0I, a11ows Counsel to continue to fu1fiIl 
the
duties under the professional services contract.
It should also be noted that the relationship between an
attorney and his or her client, including the method of
compensation, is specifically dealt with under the Rules of
Professional Conduct. It is not the purpose of the political
Reform Act to alter such rules or the ability of governmental
agencies to seek the services of outside lega1 counsel pursuant
to professional services contracts, as specifically authorized
by Government Code Section 37103.3. These specific rules must
be given precedence over the more general rules of the
PoliticaI Reform Act.
Discussion
fmplementinq prior Governmental Decisions,
_ T!. participation by Counsel in decisions of the
City implements the previous governmental decision to
Firm for attorney services. Once the basic decision
Agency and
hire the
is made,


Fair Political Practices Commission
october 7, 1992
Page Eight
other activities should faII within the context of implementing
decisions. (Moe and Dean Letter, No. A-89-454.)
In Moe and Dean, the FPPC found the decisions to form an
assessment district, 
to give the power to levy fees and taxes,
ancl to issue bonds to be "basic decisions." In addition, the
FPPC held that the "implementing decisions" included advising
the authority as to the merits of forming an assessment
district 
under one or more procedures, obtaining requisite
notices and waivers and drafting the resolution to levy, ancl
responding to procedural questions regarding the bond issue.
The FPPC's Moe and Dean analysis did not suggest that
consultant attorney advice as to matters inherent in the
existing scope of employment with the governmental entity gives
rise to a conflict of interest. 
Although the complete Moe and
Dean contract facts were not disclosed, it appears that the
assessment district 
formation, tax levy and bond issue
questions would certainly impact the amount of work to be
performed and, therefore, the fees to be received by the law
firm under its contract as the joint powers authority
consultant. Yet, the FPPC allowed counsel without a 10 percent
ownership interest, to participate in al1 of these decisions
anil stated that the facts suggest that no counsel decisions
would have a financial effect on the firm. 
(l_d.)
Just as legal counsel can be employed to render service to
a public agency client regarding a single matter, they can also
be employed to render lega1 services on a continuing basis on a
variety of matters and to fulfill 
their professional
responsibility to properly advise their clients. 
A 1aw firm
which, pursuant to contract, serves as city attorney routinely
renders advice as to claims filed against that city. 
It could
not seriously be questioned whether that law firm could
represent the city in li.tiqation arising from the rejection of
a claim, or in negotiating a settlement with a claimant, though
in each case it is clear that the aclvice given concerning such
litigation 
would affect the fees to be received by such
attorney. 
Simj. 1arIy, it could not be argued seriously that an
attorney or firm of attorneys employed by a redevelopment
agency to provide advice on whether or not the agency shoulil
initiate 
proceedings for the adoption of a redevelopment plan
coultl not then advise the agency on the plan adoption process,
defend the agency against any legal challenge or represent the


Fair Political Practices Commission
October 7, 7992
Page Nine
agency cluring the implementation phase. The possibility that
the rendering of advice could lead to more work (and thereby
additional compensation) cannot itself disqualify a firm from
handling such additional work where the firm operates pursuant
to an "as directed" contract. A contrary rule would be
infeasibl.e and impossible to apply.
In our situation, the existing Agency,/Ci ty,/Fi rm
relationship reflects the basic decision. By their terms, both
the Agency Agreement and the City Agreement disclose that the
Agency and City contemplated a wide variety of assignments in
contracting with the Firm for legal services. The City
Atto rney,/Agency Counsel, as a member of the Firm, serves the
Agency on an "as alirected" basis and provides attorney services
solely at the Agency's or City's request. Given this
relationship, the Agency may request that the Firm do work
which ultimately leads to new work for the Firm.
Our facts are distinguishable from situations which could
arise in which legaI counsel offers services different than
those for which attorneys have particular or unique
qualifications or are outside the scope of services
contemplated by a professional services contract. The Firm is
not trying to se11 insurance to the Agency or City or promote
other kinds of servicesi rather the Agency and City hired the
Firm to provide attorney services in connection with a broad
range of issues.
For example, the Agency could request that Agency Counsel
render ailvice regarding the procedures and requirements of
creating a redevelopment project area, If the Agency makes the
determination to create the new redevelopment project area, the
Agency, in its discretion could request that the Firm, acting
principally by the Agency CounseI, be its legaI adviser
throughout the lengthy plan adoption process. A conclusion
that the Agency could not both ask the Agency Counsel for
advice regarding the viability 
of a possible redevelopment
project area and subsequently request the Agency Counsel to
oversee the plan adoption proceedings or that Agency Counsel
could not both advise the Agency on hoe, best to incur debt and
also provide bond counsel services, would severely undermine
the utility 
of a1I contracts for attorney services and would
require an unending relay of attorneys to provide the services
required by the Agency.
Similarly, conunon sense dictates that, an environmental
attorney should be able to advise a city client of the
requirements for an environmentat impact report and also, at


Fair PoLiticaI Practices Commission
October 7, 1992
Page Ten
the city's request, perform some of these tasks, including, for
example, review and comments on the draft EIR and even
defenaling the EIR against legal challenges. AIso, if 
a
contract city attorney is directed to answer Land use and
zoning questions and in the course of answering such questions
discovers defects in the city's general plan, the attorney may
notify the city of the defect and, at the city's request,
provide legal services to correct the defective general p1an.
Further, a contract city attorney could recommend the city seek
FPPC advice regarding a problem anal at the city's direction
prepare the request.
The FPPC has concluded that "a person contracting with the
county to serve as county su rveyo r-eng i neer has no conflict of
interest when he performs engineering or surveying work for the
county so long as he has no authority to determine the extent
of the work he will perform." (In re Malonev (1977) 3 FPPC
Ops. 59, at 70. )
In the Workman Advice Letter, I-87-078, the FPPC concluileal
that under the Act a county may contract with a private firm to
receive both consulting services and supplies so long as the
appropriate disinterested county officials 
make those
clecisions. (See a1so, Leidioh Advice Letter, A-89-320,)
The City Attorney/Agency Counsel, as a member of the Firm,
serves the City and the Agency on an "as clirectecl" basis. The
City ancl the Agency, not the City At to rney,/Agency Counsel or
the Firm decides whether to ask the Cj.ty At to rney,/Agency
Counsel to perform a task. In addition, neither the Eirm nor
the City At to rney./Agency Counsel have any authority to vote,
appoint, obligate or enter contracts for the City or the Agency.
Accordingly, the City and/or the Agency determines the
extent of work the City At to r ney,/Agency Counsel and the Firm
r^ri11 perform; the City Attorney/Agency Counsel does not make
that determination. As outlined above, determinations of the
Firm or the City Atto rney,/Ag ency Counsel regarding reseiich ancl
procealures to answer_various requests of the city-and/or egency
constitute implementing decisions and thus, ao not enable [rre
City At to rney./Agency Counsel or the Firm to determine the
amount of work they receive from the City and,/or tfre ggency.


Fair Potitical Practices Commission
October 7, 1992
Page Eleven
Pavments to the Firm Are Not Income.
Section 82030(b)(2) provides that "income" does not inclucle
salary ancl reimbursement for expenses received from a loca1
government agency. The Commission has previously concluded
that fees paid to an attorney who has contracted to provicle
legal services as city attorney, allency counsel and bonal
counsel should be included in the definition of "salary" for
purposes of the Act. To conclude otherwise woulcl be to prevent
governmentat agencies from contracting for Iegal services,
which was not the purpose of the Act.
The Ritchie Letter (Exhibit C) deals with a fact situation
substantially similar to the facts adalresseal in this opinion
request. The advice was sought on behalf of Mr. Siege}, who
contracteil with the City and Agency as city attorney and agency
counsel respectively and who was proposing to contract with the
Agency to provide additional services as "bond counsel." The
question presented was whether Mr, SiegeI could provide aclvice
concerning a zoning decision whi.ch was a prerequisite to a
proposed bond issue which wouLd foreseeably result in a fee
being paicl to Mr. Siegel.
After first 
acknowledging that the common clefinition of
salary would not include fees paicl to an attorney, the
Corunission conc luded :
"The purpose behind the exclusion from "income" of
salary from government agencies argues a different result,
however. If government salary were "income" under the Act,
the agency for which an official 
worked would become a
"source of income" under Section 87103, ancl the official
woulcl be disqualified from participating in any decision
which would have a material financial effect on the
agency. Clearly the policy of the Act is to avoid such a
result with respect to all government employees, not just
those who are clerical or professional and to whose public
earnings the word "salary', would be appliecl.
"On the other hanal, the policies of the Act would not
be furthereal by exempting aII ,,income,, receivecl from -
government agencies. For example, if an official 
were to
applv for a row-interesL reclevelopment roan admini"i"i"J-ty
his agency, he should be disqualiiiea fiom any decision


Fair Political Practices commission
October 7, 1992
Page Twe1ve
concerning the loan. The exclusion of government salary
from "income" cannot be extended, therefore, beyond
compensation for services rendered. Payments to a retainecl
City Attorney on an hourly basis, i.e. tied to the amount
of work performed for the city, are clearly analogous to
the salary received by public employees. A percentage fee,
not being tied to the amount of work performed, is somewhat
less like a salary, but it is a customary way to compensate
lawyers for their services in connection with a bonal
issue. In addition, it is up to the agency involved to
determine the method by which they want to compensate those
who work for them, and they may believe that the chances
are such that the bond work will prove so time consuming
that the percentage fee is more favorable to the agency
than an hourly fee would be. That is a decision for the
agency to make in its fee negotiations, not one for the
Commission to make in application of the PoliticaI Reform
Act .
"In short, the fees paid to Mr. Siegel for his
services as a lawyer for the City Council anal for the
Redevelopment Agency, whether determined on an hourly or a
percentage basis, are "salary" anal are therefore excluded
from the term "income" as used throughout the Political
Reform Act."
Section 78702.1(c) of the Regulations provides that a
public official 
need not disqualify himself from participating
in a decision if the decision only affects the salary the
official 
received from a local governmental entity. 
This
section is a further recognition of the concept addressed in
the Ritchie Letter: that a pubtic official 
shoulil not be
disqualified from participating in a decision merely as the
result of the receipt of a fee for providing such advice.
In the present case, the only di.fference in the facts from
those aaldressed in the Ritchie Letter is that the fees are paid
to the Firm and not to counsel. unress such fees are rikewise
excruded as sarary for purposes of determining wrrein"r-oi-not "
decision of the City or Agency will have a ma[eria] affect on
the Firm as a source of income to counsel, the commisiio"-*o"ra
be left with the absurd result ttrat Counset carrnof piiti"ip"t"
in a decision which resutts in a fee being paid t" ih;-ii;;;
while Counsel could participate in s"cr,-aecision-ir-f;"-;;;,
were paid directly to Counsel.


Fair Political Practices commission
october 7, 1992
Page Thi rteen
Statutes are to be interpreted in such a manner so as to
carry out the purpose of the statute, while avoiding absurd
results. 
The rationale unclerlying the Ritchie Letter achieves
this result and should be applied in the present case. To do
otherwise would be to bar public agencies from obtaining
specialized lega] services from outside counsel on a
contractual basis and that i.s not the purpose of the Act.
Ritchie Advice Letter )
( See
Counsel's Participation Is Leqallv Required.
Section 87101 dictates that Counsel is not requireil to
disqualify himself from participating in a ilecision for which
the Firm will receive a fee, if such participation is legalIy
required anct there is no alternative to such participation,
Under the facts presented, the Agency and the City have
contracted with the Firm to provide legaI services in
connection with the City's and Agency's ongoing programs. The
Agency ancl City and the Firm have designated City
Atto rney,/Agency Counsel as the individual primarily responsible
for providing such lega1 services. In order for the Agency and
City to obtain the benefit of the contract, City
Atto rney,/Agency Counsel must participate in decisions of the
Agency and City by providing legal advice, negotiating and
drafting agreements and rendering written opinions upon which
the Agency and City and others may re1y. If City
At to rney,/Agency Counsel were required to disquaJ.ify himself in
each instance where participation wouLd result in a fee being
paid to the Firm, City Attorney/Agency Counsel could never
provide any services under the contract and the Agency and the
City woulil not be able to obtain legal service from City
Atto rney,/Agency CounSeI or, for that matter, any outside,
private attorney. Accordingly, there is no praLtical
alternative to this participation, barring an absurd, infinite
process wherein the City and Agency must contract with new
attorneys or firms each time a new link in the Agency,s
decisional chain is forged.
The fifth question deals with whether
participate in their private capacity and
capacity in connection with an amendirent
Counsel can
not their official
to the existing


Fair PoIitical Practices Commission
October 7, L992
Page Fourteen
contracts between the City,/Agency and the firm. 
The answer to
this question is clear and is found in Section 18700(d)(2) &
(3) of the FPPC Regulations wherein it states:
"(d) Making or participating in the making
of a governmental decision shall not include:
"(1) Actj.ons of public officials $lhich are
solely ministerial, secretarial, manual or
clerical;
" (2) Appearance by a public official 
as a
member of the general public before an agency in
the course of its prescribed governmental
function to represent himself or herself on
matters related solely to his or her personal
interests i or
"(3) Actions by public officials,
employees, or employees, or employee
representatives relating to their compensation or
the terms or conditions of their employment or
contract. "
The actions contemplated by the fifth question are actions
of Counsel (a public official) 
relating to the terms of their
emplo)rment contract or actions of a public official 
as a member
of the general public relating solely to Counsel's personal
interests. 
In either event, since Counsel does not make or
participate in governmental decisions within the meaning of the
Act, such participation in a private capacity does not violate
the Act .
Conclualinq Rema r ks

sections.question:
was whether Mr, SiegeI could provide aclvice
concerning a zoning decision whi.ch was a prerequisite to a
proposed bond issue which wouLd foreseeably result in a fee
being paicl to Mr. Siegel.
After first 
acknowledging that the common clefinition of
salary would not include fees paicl to an attorney, the
Corunission conc luded :
"The purpose behind the exclusion from "income" of
salary from government agencies argues a different result,
however. If government salary were "income" under the Act,
the agency for which an official 
worked would become a
"source of income" under Section 87103, ancl the official
woulcl be disqualified from participating in any decision
which would have a material financial effect on the
agency. Clearly the policy of the Act is to avoid such a
result with respect to all government employees, not just
those who are clerical or professional and to whose public
earnings the word "salary', would be appliecl.
"On the other hanal, the policies of the Act would not
be furthereal by exempting aII ,,income,, receivecl from -
government agencies. For example, if an official 
were to
applv for a row-interesL reclevelopment roan admini"i"i"J-ty
his agency, he should be disqualiiiea fiom any decision


Fair Political Practices commission
October 7, 1992
Page Twe1ve
concerning the loan. The exclusion of government salary
from "income" cannot be extended, therefore, beyond
compensation for services rendered. Payments to a retainecl
City Attorney on an hourly basis, i.e. tied to the amount
of work performed for the city, are clearly analogous to
the salary received by public employees. A percentage fee,
not being tied to the amount of work performed, is somewhat
less like a salary, but it is a customary way to compensate
lawyers for their services in connection with a bonal
issue. In addition, it is up to the agency involved to
determine the method by which they want to compensate those
who work for them, and they may believe that the chances
are such that the bond work will prove so time consuming
that the percentage fee is more favorable to the agency
than an hourly fee would be. That is a decision for the
agency to make in its fee negotiations, not one for the
Commission to make in application of the PoliticaI Reform
Act .
"In short, the fees paid to Mr. Siegel for his
services as a lawyer for the City Council anal for the
Redevelopment Agency, whether determined on an hourly or a
percentage basis, are "salary" anal are therefore excluded
from the term "income" as used throughout the Political
Reform Act."
Section 78702.1(c) of the Regulations provides that a
public official 
need not disqualify himself from participating
in a decision if the decision only affects the salary the
official 
received from a local governmental entity. 
This
section is a further recognition of the concept addressed in
the Ritchie Letter: that a pubtic official 
shoulil not be
disqualified from participating in a decision merely as the
result of the receipt of a fee for providing such advice.
In the present case, the only di.fference in the facts from
those aaldressed in the Ritchie Letter is that the fees are paid
to the Firm and not to counsel. unress such fees are rikewise
excruded as sarary for purposes of determining wrrein"r-oi-not "

be left with the absurd result ttrat Counset carrnof piiti"ip"t"

Statutes are to be interpreted in such a manner so as to
carry out the purpose of the statute, while avoiding absurd
results. 
The rationale unclerlying the Ritchie Letter achieves
this result and should be applied in the present case. To do
otherwise would be to bar public agencies from obtaining

Counsel's Participation Is Leqallv Required.
Section 87101 dictates that Counsel is not requireil to
disqualify himself from participating in a ilecision for which
the Firm will receive a fee, if such participation is legalIy
required anct there is no alternative to such participation,
Under the facts presented, the Agency and the City have
contracted with the Firm to provide legaI services in
connection with the City's and Agency's ongoing programs. The
Agency ancl City and the Firm have designated City
Atto rney,/Agency Counsel as the individual primarily responsible
for providing such lega1 services. In order for the Agency and
City to obtain the benefit of the contract, City
Atto rney,/Agency Counsel must participate in decisions of the
Agency and City by providing legal advice, negotiating and
drafting agreements and rendering written opinions upon which
the Agency and City and others may re1y. If City
At to rney,/Agency Counsel were required to disquaJ.ify himself in
each instance where participation wouLd result in a fee being
paid to the Firm, City Attorney/Agency Counsel could never
provide any services under the contract and the Agency and the
City woulil not be able to obtain legal service from City
Atto rney,/Agency CounSeI or, for that matter, any outside,
private attorney. Accordingly, there is no praLtical
alternative to this participation, barring an absurd, infinite
process wherein the City and Agency must contract with new
attorneys or firms each time a new link in the Agency,s
decisional chain is forged.
The fifth question deals with whether
participate in their private capacity and
capacity in connection with an amendirent
Counsel can
not their official
to the existing


Fair PoIitical Practices Commission
October 7, L992
Page Fourteen
contracts between the City,/Agency and the firm. 
The answer to
this question is clear and is found in Section 18700(d)(2) &
(3) of the FPPC Regulations wherein it states:
"(d) Making or participating in the making
of a governmental decision shall not include:
"(1) Actj.ons of public officials $lhich are
solely ministerial, secretarial, manual or
clerical;
" (2) Appearance by a public official 
as a
member of the general public before an agency in
the course of its prescribed governmental
function to represent himself or herself on
matters related solely to his or her personal
interests i or
"(3) Actions by public officials,
employees, or employees, or employee
representatives relating to their compensation or
the terms or conditions of their employment or
contract. "
The actions contemplated by the fifth question are actions
of Counsel (a public official) 
relating to the terms of their
emplo)rment contract or actions of a public official 
as a member
of the general public relating solely to Counsel's personal
interests. 
In either event, since Counsel does not make or
participate in governmental decisions within the meaning of the
Act, such participation in a private capacity does not violate
the Act .

sections.analysis:
fmplementinq prior Governmental Decisions,
_ T!. participation by Counsel in decisions of the
City implements the previous governmental decision to
Firm for attorney services. Once the basic decision
Agency and
hire the
is made,


Fair Political Practices Commission
october 7, 1992
Page Eight
other activities should faII within the context of implementing
decisions. (Moe and Dean Letter, No. A-89-454.)
In Moe and Dean, the FPPC found the decisions to form an
assessment district, 
to give the power to levy fees and taxes,
ancl to issue bonds to be "basic decisions." In addition, the
FPPC held that the "implementing decisions" included advising
the authority as to the merits of forming an assessment
district 
under one or more procedures, obtaining requisite
notices and waivers and drafting the resolution to levy, ancl
responding to procedural questions regarding the bond issue.
The FPPC's Moe and Dean analysis did not suggest that
consultant attorney advice as to matters inherent in the
existing scope of employment with the governmental entity gives
rise to a conflict of interest. 
Although the complete Moe and
Dean contract facts were not disclosed, it appears that the
assessment district 
formation, tax levy and bond issue
questions would certainly impact the amount of work to be
performed and, therefore, the fees to be received by the law
firm under its contract as the joint powers authority
consultant. Yet, the FPPC allowed counsel without a 10 percent
ownership interest, to participate in al1 of these decisions
anil stated that the facts suggest that no counsel decisions
would have a financial effect on the firm. 
(l_d.)
Just as legal counsel can be employed to render service to
a public agency client regarding a single matter, they can also
be employed to render lega1 services on a continuing basis on a
variety of matters and to fulfill 
their professional
responsibility to properly advise their clients. 
A 1aw firm
which, pursuant to contract, serves as city attorney routinely
renders advice as to claims filed against that city. 
It could
not seriously be questioned whether that law firm could
represent the city in li.tiqation arising from the rejection of
a claim, or in negotiating a settlement with a claimant, though
in each case it is clear that the aclvice given concerning such
litigation 
would affect the fees to be received by such
attorney. 
Simj. 1arIy, it could not be argued seriously that an
attorney or firm of attorneys employed by a redevelopment
agency to provide advice on whether or not the agency shoulil
initiate 
proceedings for the adoption of a redevelopment plan
coultl not then advise the agency on the plan adoption process,
defend the agency against any legal challenge or represent the


Fair Political Practices Commission
October 7, 7992
Page Nine
agency cluring the implementation phase. The possibility that
the rendering of advice could lead to more work (and thereby
additional compensation) cannot itself disqualify a firm from
handling such additional work where the firm operates pursuant
to an "as directed" contract. A contrary rule would be
infeasibl.e and impossible to apply.
In our situation, the existing Agency,/Ci ty,/Fi rm
relationship reflects the basic decision. By their terms, both
the Agency Agreement and the City Agreement disclose that the
Agency and City contemplated a wide variety of assignments in
contracting with the Firm for legal services. The City
Atto rney,/Agency Counsel, as a member of the Firm, serves the
Agency on an "as alirected" basis and provides attorney services
solely at the Agency's or City's request. Given this
relationship, the Agency may request that the Firm do work
which ultimately leads to new work for the Firm.
Our facts are distinguishable from situations which could
arise in which legaI counsel offers services different than
those for which attorneys have particular or unique
qualifications or are outside the scope of services
contemplated by a professional services contract. The Firm is
not trying to se11 insurance to the Agency or City or promote
other kinds of servicesi rather the Agency and City hired the
Firm to provide attorney services in connection with a broad
range of issues.
For example, the Agency could request that Agency Counsel
render ailvice regarding the procedures and requirements of
creating a redevelopment project area, If the Agency makes the
determination to create the new redevelopment project area, the
Agency, in its discretion could request that the Firm, acting
principally by the Agency CounseI, be its legaI adviser
throughout the lengthy plan adoption process. A conclusion
that the Agency could not both ask the Agency Counsel for
advice regarding the viability 
of a possible redevelopment
project area and subsequently request the Agency Counsel to
oversee the plan adoption proceedings or that Agency Counsel
could not both advise the Agency on hoe, best to incur debt and
also provide bond counsel services, would severely undermine
the utility 
of a1I contracts for attorney services and would
require an unending relay of attorneys to provide the services
required by the Agency.
Similarly, conunon sense dictates that, an environmental
attorney should be able to advise a city client of the
requirements for an environmentat impact report and also, at


Fair PoLiticaI Practices Commission
October 7, 1992
Page Ten
the city's request, perform some of these tasks, including, for
example, review and comments on the draft EIR and even
defenaling the EIR against legal challenges. AIso, if 
a
contract city attorney is directed to answer Land use and
zoning questions and in the course of answering such questions
discovers defects in the city's general plan, the attorney may
notify the city of the defect and, at the city's request,
provide legal services to correct the defective general p1an.
Further, a contract city attorney could recommend the city seek
FPPC advice regarding a problem anal at the city's direction
prepare the request.
The FPPC has concluded that "a person contracting with the
county to serve as county su rveyo r-eng i neer has no conflict of
interest when he performs engineering or surveying work for the
county so long as he has no authority to determine the extent
of the work he will perform." (In re Malonev (1977) 3 FPPC
Ops. 59, at 70. )
In the Workman Advice Letter, I-87-078, the FPPC concluileal
that under the Act a county may contract with a private firm to
receive both consulting services and supplies so long as the
appropriate disinterested county officials 
make those
clecisions. (See a1so, Leidioh Advice Letter, A-89-320,)
The City Attorney/Agency Counsel, as a member of the Firm,
serves the City and the Agency on an "as clirectecl" basis. The
City ancl the Agency, not the City At to rney,/Agency Counsel or
the Firm decides whether to ask the Cj.ty At to rney,/Agency
Counsel to perform a task. In addition, neither the Eirm nor
the City At to rney./Agency Counsel have any authority to vote,
appoint, obligate or enter contracts for the City or the Agency.
Accordingly, the City and/or the Agency determines the
extent of work the City At to r ney,/Agency Counsel and the Firm
r^ri11 perform; the City Attorney/Agency Counsel does not make
that determination. As outlined above, determinations of the
Firm or the City Atto rney,/Ag ency Counsel regarding reseiich ancl
procealures to answer_various requests of the city-and/or egency
constitute implementing decisions and thus, ao not enable [rre
City At to rney./Agency Counsel or the Firm to determine the
amount of work they receive from the City and,/or tfre ggency.


Fair Potitical Practices Commission
October 7, 1992
Page Eleven
Pavments to the Firm Are Not Income.
Section 82030(b)(2) provides that "income" does not inclucle
salary ancl reimbursement for expenses received from a loca1
government agency. The Commission has previously concluded
that fees paid to an attorney who has contracted to provicle
legal services as city attorney, allency counsel and bonal
counsel should be included in the definition of "salary" for
purposes of the Act. To conclude otherwise woulcl be to prevent
governmentat agencies from contracting for Iegal services,
which was not the purpose of the Act.
The Ritchie Letter (Exhibit C) deals with a fact situation
substantially similar to the facts adalresseal in this opinion
request. The advice was sought on behalf of Mr. Siege}, who
contracteil with the City and Agency as city attorney and agency
counsel respectively and who was proposing to contract with the
Agency to provide additional services as "bond counsel." The

LUBOW L^WRENCE E, COBN a= c? <^c, Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, California 95814 Gent lemen : The City of Lancaster and the Lancaster Redevelopment Agency are currently undertaking the implementation of redevelopment plans for six separate redevelopment projects which will require negotiating, drafting and approving agreements with developers anil property owners for the development of property within the redevelopment project areas, the acquisition of real property through negotiation or eminent clomain proceedings and the issuance of bonds, notes or other forms of indebtedness to provide the necessary funds for such redevelopment activities. In addition the City and Agency have initiatecl proceedings for the adoption of a redevelopment plan for a seventh redevelopment project area. The City and the Agency have entered into contracts for Iegal services with the Iaw firm of Stradling, Yocca, Carlson & Rauth (the "Firm"). while the legaI services are provided by a number of attorneys employed by the Firm, the Firm and the Agency,/City have agreecl that David McEwen, a shareholder in the Firm, wilI be primarily responsible for providing such services and wilI act as City Attorney and Agency Counsel. The attorneys provi.ding Iegal services hereunder are collectively referred to as "CounseI". AlI fees are paid directly to the Firm. On behalf of the City of Lancaster, the Lancaster Realevelopment Agency and ourselves, we are requesting a formal opinion on the following questions: ATTORNEYS AT LAW Fair PoIitical Practices commission October 7, 1992 Page Two 1. May Counsel participate in a governmental decision by provicling advice to the City./Agency or by otherwise providing Iegal services pursuant to the existing professional services contracts between the City,/Agency anal the Firm in connection with the drafting anil negotiating of agreements relating to the implementation of the redevelopment projects, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the Firm wi]1 receive a fee for such services? 2. May Counsel participate in a governmental ilecision by provicling advice to the Ci.ty,/Agency or by otherwise providing legal services pursuant to the existing professional services contracts in connection with the proceedings for the acloption of a proposed redevelopment project area, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the Firm will be asked to provide aclclitional legal services in connection with the implementation of the redevelopment plan? 3. May Counsel participate in a governmental clecision by proviCling advice to the CiLy,/Agency or by otherwise provicling legaI advice pursuant to the existing professional services contracts concerning threatened or pencling litigation, if it
